question: A manufacturer of paper products wants to compare the variation in daily production levels at two paper mills. Independent random samples of days are selected from each mill, and the production levels (in units) are recorded. The data are shown in Table 8.8. Do these data provide sufficient evidence to indicate a difference in the variability of production levels at the two paper mills? (Use $\alpha=.10$.)
data:
\begin{tabular}{l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l}
\hline Table 8.8 & \multicolumn{9}{|l}{ Production Levels at Two Paper Mills } \\
\hline Mill 1: & 34 & 18 & 28 & 21 & 40 & 23 & 29 & \\
& 25 & 10 & 38 & 32 & 22 & 22 & & \\
\hline Mill 2: & 31 & 13 & 27 & 19 & 22 & 18 & 23 & 21\\
& 18 & 15 & 24 & 13 & 19 & 18 & 23 & 13\\
\hline
\end{tabular}
answer:
Let $$
\begin{aligned}
& \sigma_1^2=\text { Population variance of production levels at mill } 1 \\
& \sigma_2^2=\text { Population variance of production levels at mill } 2
\end{aligned}
$$


The hypotheses of interest, then, are

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
H_0: \frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2}=1 & \left(\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2\right) \\
H_a: \frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2} \neq 1 & \left(\sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2\right)
\end{array}
$$

The nature of the $F$-tables given in Appendix D: Tables affects the form of the test statistic. To form the rejection region for a two-tailed $F$-test, we want to make certain that the upper tail is used because only the upper-tail values of $F$ are shown in the tables in Appendix D: Tables. To accomplish this, we will always place the larger sample variance in the numerator of the F-test statistic. This doubles the tabulated value for $\alpha$ because we double the probability that the $F$-ratio will fall in the upper tail by always placing the larger sample variance in the numerator-that is, we establish a one-tailed rejection region by putting the larger variance in the numerator rather than establishing rejection regions in both tails.
Thus, for our example, we have a numerator $s_1^2$ with df $=\nu_1=n_1-1=12$ and a denominator $s_2^2$ with $\mathrm{df}=\nu_2=n_2-1=17$. Therefore, the test statistic will be

$$
F=\frac{\text { Larger sample variance }}{\text { Smaller sample variance }}=\frac{s_1^2}{s_2^2}
$$

and we will reject $H_0: \sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2$ for $\alpha=.10$ when the calculated value of $F$ exceeds the tabulated value:

$$
F_{\alpha / 2}=F_{05}=2.38
$$

(see Figure 8.19)
To calculate the value of the test statistic, we require the sample variances. Summary statistics for the data in Table 8.8 are shown on the Minitab printout, Figure 8.20. The sample standard deviations (shaded) are $s_1=8.36$ and $s_2=4.85$. Therefore,

$$
F=\frac{s_1^2}{s_2^2}=\frac{(8.36)^2}{(4.85)^2}=2.97
$$


When we compare this result to the rejection region shown in Figure 8.19, we see that $F=2.97$ falls in the rejection region. Therefore, the data provide sufficient evidence to indicate that the population variances differ. It appears that the variation in production levels at mill 1 tends to be higher than the variation at mill 2 .

meta_info.caption: An F-Test ApplicationComparing Paper Mill Production Variation
meta_info.section: 8.6 Comparing Two Population Variances: Independent Sampling
